attributes.stem:
A three-year-old girl presents to the Emergency Department with epistaxis and gum bleeding for two hours. She has had multiple episodes of easy bruising and prolonged bleeding after minor cuts since early childhood. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her family history is significant for a bleeding disorder affecting her maternal aunt.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are scattered bruises of varying age on her limbs. Laboratory investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 115 g/L (110-140)

  • Platelet count 280 × 10⁹/L (150-400)

  • Prothrombin time 12 seconds (11-13)

  • Activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) 30 seconds (25-35)

  • von Willebrand factor antigen 95% (50-160)

  • Ristocetin cofactor assay: normal
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying pathophysiology?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a lifelong history of mucocutaneous bleeding, normal platelet count, and normal coagulation studies including von Willebrand studies. The family history suggests an inherited disorder. These features are classic for Glanzmann thrombasthenia, which is caused by platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa deficiency. This complex is essential for platelet aggregation through fibrinogen binding.

Anti-platelet antibodies cause immune thrombocytopenia (ITP), which would present with a low platelet count. The normal platelet count in this case rules out ITP. Additionally, ITP typically has an acute onset rather than a lifelong history.

Von Willebrand disease can present similarly with mucocutaneous bleeding, but would show reduced von Willebrand factor levels. The normal von Willebrand factor antigen and ristocetin cofactor levels make this diagnosis very unlikely.

Factor VIII deficiency (haemophilia A) typically presents with deep tissue and joint bleeding rather than mucocutaneous bleeding. It would also show a prolonged APTT.

Factor IX deficiency (haemophilia B) presents similarly to Factor VIII deficiency with deep tissue and joint bleeding, and would also show a prolonged APTT. The normal coagulation studies in this case rule out both haemophilia A and B.

Glanzmann thrombasthenia is an autosomal recessive disorder. The diagnosis can be confirmed by flow cytometry showing absent or reduced expression of glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex on platelets. Treatment involves platelet transfusion for significant bleeding episodes and avoidance of antiplatelet medications.
learningPoints: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is caused by deficiency or dysfunction of platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex.
Glanzmann thrombasthenia presents with mucocutaneous bleeding from early childhood.
Normal platelet count, coagulation studies, and von Willebrand studies with mucocutaneous bleeding suggest a platelet function defect.
references: Nurden P, Stritt S, Favier R, Nurden AT. Inherited platelet diseases with normal platelet count: phenotypes, genotypes and diagnostic strategy. Haematologica. 2021;106(2):337-350. Published 2021 Feb 1. doi:10.3324/haematol.2020.248153
